elements.section-2411-003: Linear orders and splitting into intervals
elements.sent-2411-127: Our aim in this section is to prove an important technical lemma about linear orderings.
elements.sent-2411-128: This lemma describes how to take a formula that is about a tuple of elements in a linear ordering and find a stronger formula that only refers to particular intervals inside of the linear ordering.
elements.sent-2411-129: In essence, the lemma helps us eliminate parameters from formulas about linear orderings in a manner that is particularly expedient for the proof of the main theorem.
elements.sent-2411-130: We begin by introducing some notation.
elements.sent-2411-131: Given a linear order $\mc{A}$ and a tuple $\bar{a = (a_1,\ldots,a_n) \in \mc{A}$, listed in increasing order, we will frequently split $\mc{A}$ up into the intervals $(-\infty,a_1),(a_1,a_2),\ldots,(a_n,\infty)$.}
elements.sent-2411-132: Throughout the paper, will make use of this division into intervals, and so we will adopt the convention that we use $a_0$ for $-\infty$ and $a_{n+1}$ for $\infty$; thus, the intervals $(a_i,a_{i+1 )$ for $i = 0,\ldots,n$ are the intervals into which $\bar{a}$ splits $\mc{A}$.}
elements.sent-2411-133: There will be a few places where we define notation to take advantage of this so that we do not need to split into cases based on whether or not an interval has a bound at infinity.
elements.sent-2411-134: Given an interval $(a,b)$ in a linear order $\mc{A}$, $(a,b)$ is a linear order in its own right, and hence we can write $(a,b) \models \varphi$ for a sentence $\varphi$.
elements.sent-2411-135: The fact that $\varphi$ is true in $(a,b)$ can also be witnessed in $\mc{A}$ by relativizing the formula $\varphi$ to the interval $(a,b)$.
elements.sent-2411-136: Let $\varphi \Res_{(x,y)}$ be the formula in two variables, which relativizes all of the quantifiers of $\varphi$ to the interval $(x,y)$.. Then \begin{equation}\mc{A} \models \varphi \Res_{(a,b)} \;\; \Longleftrightarrow (a,b) \models \varphi.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-137: The quantifier complexity of the relativized formula will be the same as that of the original formula.
elements.sent-2411-138: We can also relativize $\varphi$ to intervals $(-\infty,x)$ and $(y,\infty)$.
elements.sent-2411-139: This will be useful using the convention above that given elements $a_1 < \ldots < a_n$, we let $a_0$ represent $-\infty$ and $a_{n+1}$ represent $\infty$, so that, e.g., given sentences $\theta_0,\ldots,\theta_n$, \begin{equation}\mc{A} \models \bigwedge_{i=0}^n \theta_i \Res_{(a_i,a_{i+1})}\end{equation} expresses that after dividing $\mc{A}$ into $n+1$ intervals $(-\infty,a_1),(a_1,a_2),\ldots,(a_n,\infty)$, each interval satisfies the corresponding sentence $\theta_i$.
elements.sent-2411-140: Note that $\varphi \Res_{(-\infty,x)}$ and $\varphi \Res_{(y,\infty)}$ are formulas with only one free variable.

elements.section-2411-006: Examples of Scott Skipping in Linear Orderings
elements.sent-2411-423: Our main result indicates that the complexity of a theory of linear orderings and its simplest model is small and finite.
elements.sent-2411-424: In this section, we construct some examples of a non-trivial gap between the complexity of a theory of linear orderings and its simplest model.
elements.sent-2411-425: In particular, we construct examples at limit and non-limit levels of $\Pi_\alpha$ sentences with structures of Scott rank at least $\alpha+1$.
elements.sent-2411-426: This means that linear orderings do not have the strongest tameness property once conjectured in Question \ref{question:BIRS (i.e. that all $\Pi_\alpha$ sentences have a model of Scott rank $\alpha$).}
elements.sent-2411-427: We demonstrate examples of this phenomenon at limit ordinals and away from limit ordinals.
elements.sent-2411-428: In the subsequent section, we will demonstrate that we can take $\alpha$ to be essentially any countable ordinal by iterating our constructions up the hyperarithmetic hierarchy.
elements.sent-2411-429: We do not construct an example that is optimal in the sense that it is exactly an $\E_\alpha$ sentence with models of Scott rank at least $\alpha+2$.
elements.sent-2411-430: This is left as an open question.
elements.sent-2411-431: In \cite{GHTH the authors together with Ho give constructions for linear orders of Scott complexity $\Sigma_{\lambda + 1}$ where $\lambda$ is a limit ordinal.}
elements.sent-2411-432: Moreover, there are structures $\mc{A}$ such that all $\mc{B \equiv_\lambda \mc{A}$ have Scott complexity $\Sigma_{\lambda + 1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-433: By taking an $\omega$-sum of one of these examples, one gets:.
elements.sent-2411-434: There is a $\Pi_\lambda$ theory $T$ extending the axioms of linear orders such that no model of $T_\lambda$ has a $\Sigma_{\lambda + 2}$ Scott sentence.
elements.sent-2411-435: In particular, all models of $T_\lambda$ have Scott rank $\geq \lambda + 1$.
